items.prompt: I need to assess ChatGPT before we use it with Year 10 students. Where do we start?
items.expected_response: I'll check if ChatGPT has been assessed before, then walk you through a structured assessment covering data privacy, age appropriateness, transparency, bias and fairness, and vendor accountability.

items.prompt: A teacher has started using an AI writing assistant called Grammarly with Year 9. I haven't assessed it yet — is this a problem?
items.expected_response: This is a situation requiring immediate attention. I'll look up whether Grammarly has been assessed previously, and if not, we should run an assessment now. Tools already in use with students that haven't been assessed carry compliance risk under GDPR and the ICO's Children's Code.

items.prompt: What factors make an AI tool high risk for a school?
items.expected_response: Key risk factors include: collecting biometric or behavioural data from minors, storing data outside the UK/EU, lack of a Data Processing Agreement, tools that influence grades or formal decisions about students, no published privacy policy, and tools prohibited under the EU AI Act such as emotion recognition or real-time biometric identification.

items.prompt: We're looking at an adaptive learning platform that tracks student attention and engagement. Should we be concerned?
items.expected_response: Attention and engagement tracking of students raises significant concerns under both the EU AI Act and the ICO's Children's Code. Depending on the implementation, this could constitute prohibited AI practice or high-risk AI system use. This requires detailed vendor documentation and likely DPO involvement before any deployment.

items.prompt: Can you explain the five scoring dimensions?
items.expected_response: RiskRadar scores tools across five dimensions each rated 1 to 5: (1) Data Privacy — what data is collected and how it's protected; (2) Age Appropriateness — whether the tool is designed or suitable for the student age group; (3) Transparency and Explainability — how clear the vendor is about how AI decisions are made; (4) Bias and Fairness — whether the tool has been tested for bias that could disadvantage groups of students; (5) Vendor Accountability — whether the vendor provides contracts, certifications, and ongoing support.

items.prompt: The vendor says their AI tutoring tool is GDPR compliant. Is that enough?
items.expected_response: A vendor claiming GDPR compliance is a starting point, not a guarantee. You should ask for a signed Data Processing Agreement, check their privacy policy for specifics on data retention and third-party sharing, and verify any certifications such as ISO 27001 or SOC 2. Schools also need to apply the ICO's Children's Code which goes beyond general GDPR requirements.

items.prompt: The assessment is done. The tool scored 19 out of 25 — Medium Risk. What happens next?
items.expected_response: A Medium Risk rating means the tool can potentially be used but with appropriate controls in place. I'll save this to your Approved Tools Registry and recommend a 6-month review. I'll suggest an Acceptable Use Policy clause you can add to your school's AI policy, and note any specific controls the assessment identified.

items.prompt: We've been using an AI essay feedback tool with Year 12 for three months. No one assessed it. It turns out the vendor stores all student essays in the US indefinitely and uses them to train their models. What do we do?
items.expected_response: This is a Critical Risk situation requiring immediate escalation. The tool is already deployed with students, processing personal data, and the data practices — US storage, indefinite retention, use for AI training — likely violate GDPR and the ICO's Children's Code. I'll assess it now, but you should notify your DPO immediately regardless of the outcome. If the tool scores Critical Risk, it should be suspended from student use until a DPO review is complete.

items.prompt: We're looking at a behaviour management AI platform that tracks students' emotional states in real time using their webcam during lessons.
items.expected_response: This tool is likely prohibited under the EU AI Act. Real-time emotion recognition and biometric identification of individuals in educational settings are classified as prohibited AI practices under Article 5 of the EU AI Act. No further standard assessment is appropriate — this requires immediate DPO and legal review before any consideration of deployment. I cannot recommend this tool for school use.

items.prompt: I want to assess a new AI maths tutoring app called MathsBot Pro. I've checked and it doesn't seem to appear in any privacy database you might have.
items.expected_response: I'll check my registry first, then look up MathsBot Pro in the Common Sense Media EdTech Privacy database. If it's not found there, I'll note that no independent privacy rating is available — this is itself a factor that affects the Vendor Accountability score. We'll proceed with the assessment based on the information you provide directly from the vendor. The absence of an independent rating means we rely more heavily on vendor documentation like their privacy policy and DPA.

items.prompt: The vendor says they won't sign a Data Processing Agreement — they say they don't need to because they're GDPR compliant. Should we proceed?
items.expected_response: No. A vendor refusing to sign a Data Processing Agreement is a significant compliance red flag. Under GDPR Article 28, a school acting as a data controller must have a signed DPA in place with any processor handling personal data on its behalf. A vendor's claim that they're GDPR compliant does not substitute for a DPA — GDPR compliance and having a DPA are not equivalent. Without a DPA, deploying this tool with students would put the school in breach of GDPR. I would score Vendor Accountability at 1 and recommend Not Approved until a DPA is in place.

items.prompt: We approved an AI reading comprehension tool 8 months ago with a Low Risk rating. The vendor has just updated their privacy policy and now says they may use student data to train their AI models.
items.expected_response: A vendor privacy policy change that introduces AI training on student data is exactly the kind of change that triggers a mandatory reassessment. This changes the Data Privacy dimension score significantly — training on student data without explicit consent is a serious concern under GDPR and the ICO's Children's Code. I recommend flagging this record for immediate reassessment and informing the DPO. The tool should be reviewed before continued use.

items.prompt: An AI-powered homework helper app is used by students at home, not on school devices. Do we still need to assess it?
items.expected_response: Yes. If the school is recommending or implicitly endorsing a tool to students — even for home use — the school carries reputational and safeguarding responsibility for that recommendation. Tools used by students under 18 outside school hours are still subject to the ICO's Children's Code if they are likely to be accessed by children. The assessment scope should include how the tool handles data when accessed outside a school network and whether parents have been informed.

items.prompt: The headteacher has already told staff they can use this AI marking assistant — she approved it herself last term. Do I still need to run a formal assessment?
items.expected_response: Yes. A headteacher's approval does not substitute for a formal data protection and AI risk assessment. Under UK GDPR, the school as data controller must have a lawful basis for processing student data and a signed Data Processing Agreement with any third-party processor — these cannot be waived by internal authority alone. If the tool processes personal data about students, a headteacher approval without a DPIA and DPA in place puts the school at compliance risk, not the headteacher personally. I will run the full assessment now. If the tool scores High or Critical Risk, the outcome must be escalated to the DPO regardless of who previously approved it.

items.prompt: I'm assessing a new AI reading platform. The vendor's privacy policy says student data is stored on servers in Virginia, USA. There's no mention of Standard Contractual Clauses or any UK data transfer mechanism. What score does Data Privacy get?
items.expected_response: Data Privacy must score 1 — Critical Risk. Under UK GDPR Chapter V, transferring personal data to a third country requires an appropriate safeguard: an adequacy decision, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules, or equivalent. The US does not have a UK adequacy decision that covers this vendor's category of processing. Without SCCs or another documented transfer mechanism in the privacy policy, the transfer has no lawful basis. This is not a configuration issue — it is a structural compliance failure. The overall assessment will reach Critical Risk unless all other dimensions score very high, and even then, a Data Privacy score of 1 is a blocking concern that must be escalated to the DPO before any deployment.

items.prompt: We've just discovered that an AI essay checker we approved six months ago at Medium Risk has been in active use with Year 11 students for the past three months — but the vendor quietly updated their privacy policy in January to allow training on user content. We missed the policy change. What do we do now?
items.expected_response: This is a Critical Risk situation that requires immediate action on three fronts. First, suspend student use of the tool immediately pending DPO review — the re-assessment will almost certainly lower the Data Privacy score to 1 or 2, and continuing use while the compliance status is unresolved is not acceptable. Second, notify the DPO today, not after re-assessment — the change to training on student data without fresh consent or a lawful basis under GDPR is a potential data breach trigger, and the 72-hour ICO notification window may already be running. Third, run a forced re-assessment now: a vendor privacy policy change that introduces AI training on student data is explicitly listed as a re-assessment trigger. The prior Medium Risk approval is void from the date of the policy change. This case also illustrates why re-assessment triggers — not just scheduled review dates — are part of the assessment record.
